original work is properly cited.
© 2026 The Author(s). Engineering Reports published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.
The late-stage presentation is a major factor that contributes to
elevated mortality rates of breast cancer cases. Quite a number of
women receive diagnoses in the advanced stages due to limited
access to diagnostic and screening services [5, 6]. Therefore, early
diagnosis of breast cancer has become the key link in the whole
management of breast cancer that helps to significantly reduce
the mortality rate.
Invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) is the most prevalent histologi-
cal type of breast cancer, accounting for approximately 75%–81%
of cases, invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) is considered diagnos-
tically challenging and is ranked as the second most common
type, representing about 5%–15% of breast cancer cases [7–9].
Engineering Reports, 2026; 8:e70655
1 of 19
https://doi.org/10.1002/eng2.70655

Rare malignant subtypes such as mucinous carcinoma have a
mixture of mucinous and non-mucinous (like ductal), while rare
benign histopathology subtypes such as tubular adenomas are
often misdiagnosed as fibroadenomas due to their similar clinical
presentation [10, 11].
Traditional diagnostic methods, such as mammography, are
expensive and usually increase labor costs because they rely
on radiologists for large-scale screening [12]. The rise of artifi-
cial intelligence (AI) and deep learning provides a powerful and
cost efficient alternative to automating and improving diagnostic
accuracy [13, 14]. Moreover, AI has shown a significant potential
to improve diagnostic accuracy and speed [14].
Convolutional
networks
(ConvNets)
such
as
ResNet
and
DenseNet have become a cornerstone in the field of image
classification due to their ability to learn local patterns and
extract hierarchical features from raw pixel images. This capa-
bility allows them to effectively handle complex visual tasks,
making them a preferred choice for image classification in vari-
ous domains [15, 16]. Although ConvNets have proven effective
in image-based classification tasks, they often lack global context
modeling capabilities to capture long-range dependencies, which
are crucial for discerning subtle clinical indicators in broader
regions. On the other hand, Transformers have demonstrated
strong capabilities in modeling global context via self-attention,
making them strong candidate models in general vision tasks and
potential in medical image recognition [17–19]. Despite their
strengths, transformers often lack the built-in local inductive
biases that are crucial to capture intricate medical details, such
as lesions and small-scale anomalies [20].
Hybrid architectures combining convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) and vision transformers (ViTs) have emerged as a promis-
ing approach in image classification, leveraging the strengths of
both models [21, 22]. However, these hybrid models also face
several challenges that include computational complexity, data
requirements, and discrepancies in the integration of distinct
architectural features [23]. These issues highlight the need for
efficient fusion strategies that can bridge the gap between varied
feature distributions and model performance. Some networks use
ConvNets to encode spatial inductive biases and local connectiv-
ity blocks in transformers [24, 25]. On the other hand, the Swin
Transformer has proven effective in reducing computational
cost and capturing global relationships through window-based
or shifted-window attention with hierarchical partitioning of
medical scans [26, 27]. Newer CNN variants with large kernel
sizes and inverted bottleneck blocks, such as ConvNeXt, adopt
transformer-inspired design elements from transformers to mod-
ernize classic convolutional architectures [28].
Within the healthcare domain, the simultaneous extraction of
local morphological features such as tumor boundaries, lesion
textures and broader contextual information like anatomical rela-
tionships, multi-lesion distribution is indispensable for achieving
diagnostic accuracy. Hybrid architectures that rely on parallel
branching or sequential layer integration sometimes struggle to
balance local and global information. Their shortcomings include
the integration of attention mechanisms that increase computa-
tional burden, the stacking of modules without dynamic learning,
and the failure to mimic the diagnostic reasoning of pathologists,
who typically analyze global context before refining their focus to
local features. These limitations often lead to compromised per-
formance in medical image analysis.
To address these challenges, we propose a novel hybrid
SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF, equipped with a learnable dynamic
gating fusion mechanism. Our method dynamically balances
local morphological features such tumor boundaries, lesion tex-
tures with global contextual cues like anatomical relationships,
multilesion distribution in a computationally efficient manner,
unlike prior hybrid approaches that statically stack modules or
introduce heavy attention layers. The gating module adaptively
regulates the information flow between feature streams, thereby
better mimicking the diagnostic reasoning of pathologists, who
typically analyze global context before refining their focus on
local details.
Our key contributions to this study are as follows.
• The proposed hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF enables the
adaptive integration of local and global features for improved
understanding of medical images.
• Introduced a dynamic gating mechanism that balances fea-
ture contributions across scales, thereby reducing redun-
dancy from fused modules.
• Enhanced interpretability by aligning the model focus
with pathologists’ workflow, first extracting broad con-
textual information, then refining toward critical local
morphological details.
• Our study enhanced interpretability using Local Inter-
pretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME), which high-
lighted morphological and contextual regions underpinning
predictions. Furthermore, shapley additive explanations
(SHAP) quantified the contribution of each histopatholog-
ical subtype to the overall decision making of the model.
• Achieved state-of-the-art diagnostic accuracy in multiple
histopathological subtypes of breast cancer.

Review of Literature
2.1
|
Deep Learning in Histopathology Image
Analysis
Deep learning (DL) has become an indispensable tool for
histopathological image analysis by improving the accuracy, effi-
ciency, and scalability of diagnostic processes. Unlike traditional
machine learning methods, DL leverages advanced neural net-
works to automate the diagnosis and classification of features in
histopathological images, which are substantial for diagnosing
diseases like cancer. In addition to improving diagnostic preci-
sion, the integration of DL also addresses the challenges of com-
plexity and size of histopathology images.
Neural networks are the foundation of most deep neural net-
work (DNN) algorithms, consisting of interconnected neurons
organized into layers such as input, hidden, and output lay-
ers [29]. CNNs, in particular, have substantially transformed

histopathology image analysis due to their ability to learn hier-
archical features from images. These models excel in image clas-
sification, object detection, and segmentation tasks, making them
versatile tools for histopathological analysis.
Studies have shown that models such as ResNet50, VGG16,
and DenseNet achieve high accuracy in cancer diagnosis tasks
[30, 31]. DL models have also shown the potential to improve
clinical decision-making by providing risk stratification in breast
cancer patients, offering a cost-effective alternative to molecular
profiling techniques [32]. Despite this potential, the application
of DL models in medical imaging faces substantial challenges
such as the need for large labeled datasets and extensive com-
putational resources.
Transfer learning (TL) has emerged as a solution to these
issues, allowing models to utilize pre-existing knowledge from
a source task with abundant data. TL involves fine-tuning a
pre-trained model on the target task using the limited available
data, which helps to avoid overfitting and improving performance
[33]. TL has been used effectively in histopathological images,
particularly in challenging conditions with limited data [34].
2.2
|
CNNs in Medical Image Classification
Various custom CNN-based frameworks have been utilized for
diagnosing breast cancer, focusing on high-dimensional medi-
cal image analysis. These frameworks often incorporate several
layers such as Conv2d, Maxpooling2d, Dropout, Flatten, and
Dense, achieving substantial sensitivity, specificity, and accu-
racy in results. In diagnosing breast cancer, [35] achieved an
accuracy of 86%, highlighting the effectiveness of CNN-based
models in medical imaging. Although CNNs have substantially
improved breast cancer diagnosis, challenges such as the need
for large labeled datasets, long training time, and the potential
for overfitting remain.
CNNs were implemented through TL, demonstrating signifi-
cant improvements in accuracy and objectivity while reducing
diagnosis time and variability [36]. Fine-tuning EfficientNetB0
pre-trained on ImageNet for breast cancer classification achieved
a test accuracy of 94.46%, demonstrating the model’s ability
to distinguish between malignant and non-malignant classes
[37, 38] also demonstrated substantial accuracy and reliabil-
ity of the Pre-trained DenseNet121 model compared to cus-
tom CNNs, achieving superior performance in classifying breast
cancer.
As highlighted by [39], ResNet50 consistently outperforms other
models in terms of accuracy and F-score, demonstrating excep-
tional precision and sensitivity. A modernized ResNet-50 (Con-
vNeXt), which is an architecture that integrates Swin Trans-
former training techniques and the ResNeXT model, achieved
superior results compared to other models, with an accu-
racy of 88.90%, AUC of 93.91% and F1 score of 91.21% [40].
Such improvements make ConvNeXt a promising candidate for
histopathology tasks that require efficient local feature extrac-
tion and deeper semantic representations. Despite the improve-
ments, the complexity and Variability of Histopathology images
characterized by complex morphology make it difficult for Con-
vNeXt models to capture global features effectively [41].
2.3
|
ViTs in Image Classification
Transformer models have significantly revolutionized natural
language processing (NLP) mechanisms by transitioning from
traditional sequential models to advanced architectures based on
attention mechanisms. This shift has significantly improved the
handling of long text sequences and complex dependencies, as
seen in models like BERT and GPT, which employ bidirectional
modeling to improve performance in text classification, machine
translation, and question and answer tasks [42]. In text summa-
rization, transformer models have demonstrated their robustness
to understand global context and dynamically allocate weights,
leading to improvements in both abstraction and extraction based
summarization methods. For example, Models such as PEGA-
SUS and BERT have become prominent in generating concise and
coherent summaries, showcasing the effectiveness of transformer
models [43].
The success of transformer models in NLP has given a basis
to their application to computer vision tasks. By redesigning
their network and training methods to accommodate the unique
structure of visual data, transformers have been successfully
adapted for image recognition, object detection, segmentation,
and classification tasks [44]. ViTs introduced a paradigm shift
in computer vision by treating images as sequences of tokenized
patches and applying multi-head self-attention (MSA) to model
global relationships within the image [45].
ViTs have emerged as an innovative approach in healthcare
image classification, offering substantial advantages over tradi-
tional CNNs. They have shown potential in multi-label classifi-
cation tasks, such as the identification of multiple pathologies
in chest radiographs [46]. ViTs outperformed CNN in classifying
low-quality histopathological images, achieving an accuracy of
92.8%, which was superior to the best-performing CNN model,
InceptionV3 [47, 48].

[22, 49]. The Swin Transformer introduces windowed atten-

The Swin Transformer model demonstrated superior perfor-

breast cancer mammographic images, achieving an accuracy
of 99.8% [52].
Despite remarkable performance, the absence of built-in mech-
anisms to capture local spatial hierarchies makes Swin Trans-
formers inefficient and data-intensive [22]. To mitigate these lim-
itations, hybrid models that combine CNNs and Transformers

2.4

Hybrid CNN-Swin Transformer
Architectures for Classification
Hybrid models that combine convolution neural networks with
Swin Transformers have shown considerable promise in enhanc-
ing classification tasks across various domains. These architec-
tures leverage the strengths of both CNNs and Swin Transformers
to enhance both local and global feature extraction by the convo-
lution neural network and Swin Transformer, respectively. Var-
ious studies have been conducted on different integration tech-
niques which are tailored to the specific requirements based on
the application domain.
[53] implemented graph convolutional neural networks (GCNN)
with the SWIN Transformer model (GSwTr) to improve hyper-
spectral image (HSI) classification. The study achieved an out-
standing Overall Accuracy of 99.7%, 99.9%, and 99.5%. An average
accuracy of 99.5%, 99.6%, and 98.2% for all three HSI datasets
(Pavia-University, Indian-Pines and Salinas). Hybrid-MT-ESTAN
also demonstrated remarkable performance in the classification
of breast ultrasound BUS images, achieving high accuracy, sensi-
tivity, and F1 scores of 82.7%, 86.4% and 86.0%, respectively [54].
In histopathology image classification tasks, the combination of
local and global features through separable ConvNets and patch
merging from a transformer model achieved remarkable perfor-
mance on datasets such as BACH, BreakHis, and IDC, with accu-
racies of 97.8%, 98.13% and 98.32%, respectively [55].
Despite these advances, existing hybrid models often lack effec-
tive mechanisms for multi-scale feature fusion and dynamic
adjustment of shallow versus deep feature contributions. There-
fore, new architectures are needed that incorporate learnable
dynamic gating fusion to balance efficiency, interpretability, and
accuracy in medical image classification tasks.
2.5
|
Gating Fusion Mechanisms in Medical
Image Classification
Gated fusion mechanisms have emerged as a critical compo-
nent in the classification of medical images, particularly in sce-
narios involving multimodal data. These mechanisms improve
the accuracy and robustness of medical image analysis by selec-
tively combining features from different sources, such as multiple
imaging modalities or scales. In addition, gated mechanisms pro-
vide robust performance in classification and segmentation tasks,
especially when dealing with limited labeled data or noisy inputs.
Architectures such as DPL-GFT-EFA implemented gated fusion
mechanisms to enhance the representation of medical images by
selectively highlighting important features. The model achieved
accuracies of 97.05%, 97.70%, 97.71%, 97.16%, and 97.26% on five
public datasets [56].
A gating mechanism in a new hybrid architecture that combines
CNNs with ViTs to improve the classification of lung diseases.
The study achieved a very high average classification accuracy
of 99% in multiple pulmonary conditions, including COVID-19,
Pneumonia, TB, and normal cases [57] highlighted Multi-gate
weighted fusion networks (MWFNets) for neuronal morphology
classification achieved accuracies of 91.73% and 98.18% in classi-
fying 10 and five types of neurons, respectively.
[58] proposed a gated deep reinforcement learning (GRL) model,
which achieved a substantial accuracy of 98.8% in a brain tumor
MRI dataset. In addition to the achievements in brain tumor clas-
sification, [59] employed hierarchical attentive multilevel fusion
with uncertainty quantification, which achieved classification
accuracies of 94.21%, 99.59% and 96.50% on retinal OCT, lung CT,
and chest X-ray datasets, respectively.
In medical image segmentation tasks, [60] indicated that
gate-regularized network training (GrNT) improves the perfor-
mance of widely adopted models such as U-Net and Attention
U-Net in various medical image segmentation datasets. Gated
recurrent units (GRUs) in skip connections have been shown to
improve the accuracy of multi-organ segmentation tasks, achiev-
ing a Dice coefficient of 80.66% in the Synapse dataset [61].
Transformer-based architectures such as gated axial-attention
models have been shown to outperform convolutional and other
transformer-based architectures on medical image segmenta-
tion tasks, improving over fully attention baselines by 0.92%,
4.76% and 2.72% for Brain US, GlaS and MoNuSeg datasets,
respectively [62].
This study introduces a novel and efficient hybrid architecture
for breast cancer histopathology image classification by inte-
grating the global feature extraction capabilities of the Swin
Transformer, the localized refinement power of ConvNeXt. To
further enhance the robustness and interpretability of feature
integration, the proposed hybrid model employs a learnable
dynamic gating fusion module to dynamically regulate the con-
tribution of extracted features from each branch, allowing the
model to emphasize clinically relevant patterns with trans-
parency. Our fusion mechanism not only strengthens multi-scale
representation, but also improves discriminative capability and
diagnostic accuracy across complex histopathological image
variations.
2.6
|
Motivation and Research Gaps
CNNs like ConvNeXt are highly effective in capturing local pat-
terns but often struggle to capture long-range contextual depen-
dencies which are crucial for understanding complex tissue struc-
tures. On the other hand, ViTs variants, such as Swin Trans-
former, have shown strong capabilities in modeling global depen-
dencies but lack the built-in local inductive biases that are cru-
cial for capturing intricate medical details such as lesions and
small-scale anomalies.
Several hybrid CNN Transformer models have been proposed to
bridge this performance gap. However, many still rely on static
or shallow fusion techniques that do not adequately adapt to the
complex nature of medical images. In addition, most of these
models offer limited interpretability support, making them less
suitable for real-world clinical integration, where transparency
and trust in AI decisions are paramount.

Materials and Methods
The classification of breast cancer in histopathology images poses
significant obstacles due to the intricate tissue structures, varia-
tions in cell appearance, and the presence of overlapping char-
acteristics between non-cancerous and cancerous samples. Over-
coming these challenges requires a DL framework that is both
transparent and robust to effectively identify and categorize
cancer-related patterns.
This section presents the methodology used in the development
of the hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model for histopathologi-
cal classification of breast cancer. The proposed framework inte-
grates the strengths of the Swin Transformer to capture global
features and the ConvNeXt model for local feature extraction. To
achieve robust and generalizable performance across domains,
the hybrid model is enhanced by a novel learnable dynamic gat-
ing fusion strategy. The process includes multiple stages such as
data preprocessing, dual-branch feature learning, gated fusion,
and classification, as illustrated in Figure 1.
3.1
|
Global Feature Extraction via Swin
Transformer
The preprocessed images are fed into a Swin Transformer,
an advanced DL model that captures comprehensive and
hierarchical contextual representations through window-based
self-attention and shifted windows [26]. The Swin Transformer
model employs a shifted window multi-head self-attention
(SW-MSA) mechanism which distinguishes it from vanilla trans-
formers that rely on standard MSA. The model improves compu-
tational efficiency while preserving contextual modeling through
the computation of self-attention within local non-overlapping
windows, while enabling cross-window interactions through the
shifted-window strategy. The input image is partitioned into
non-overlapping fixed size patches, with each patch represented
as a token in a transformer block. The self-attention component
of the Swin Transformer calculates the correlation of each token
with all other tokens. This enables the model to effectively cap-
ture global contextual information.
In this study, an input image is represented with original
dimensions and three color channels (RGB), then partitioned
into non-overlapping square patches. To transform vision into
sequence processing, each patch 𝐼𝑖is transformed into a sequence

Local Feature Extraction via ConvNeXt
The same input image in Equation (1) is also processed by
ConvNeXt blocks for the extraction of local features. These
blocks are specifically designed to capture fine-grained spa-
tial patterns and structural textures that may not be effectively
modeled by the global self-attention mechanisms of the Swin
Transformer. The ConvNeXt architecture consists of a sequence
of ConvNeXt blocks, each incorporating a depthwise convo-
lution with a large kernel size (7 × 7) to capture long-range
spatial dependencies. The convolutional output is subsequently
normalized, and the use of depthwise convolution signifi-
cantly reduces the number of parameters and computational
complexity [63].
The preprocessed input image is first passed through a convo-
lutional stem layer, which reduces the spatial resolution and
projects the input 𝐼∈ℝ𝐻×𝑊×𝐶into a lower-dimensional fea-
ture representation (Equation (21)). Each stage of the ConvNeXt
architecture comprises multiple ConvNeXt blocks, where each
block consists of a depthwise convolution, layer normalization,
pointwise convolutions with Gaussian error linear unit (GELU)
activation, and a residual connection (Equations (22–26)). In the
ConvNeXt branch of this study, the resulting feature tensor prop-
agates through three such stages, with each stage operating at
progressively lower spatial resolution and higher channel dimen-
sionality, as formally defined by the recursive transformation in
Equation (28).

3.5

Data Preprocessing and Training Strategy
This section outlines the experimental setup adopted for the clas-
sification of breast cancer histopathology using the BreakHis
dataset [64]. It consists of the dataset characteristics, the adopted
training strategy, and the model configurations with their corre-
sponding hyperparameters. The experiments were conducted on
the publicly available BreakHis dataset, which consists of micro-
scopic biopsy images of breast tissue collected from eight tumor
subtypes such as Adenosis, Ductal Carcinoma, Fibroadenoma,
Lobular Carcinoma, Mucinous Carcinoma, Papillary Carcinoma,
Phyllodes Tumor and Tubular Adenoma (Figure 2). The images
were provided with multiple magnification factors, for consis-
tency, we focused on a fixed resolution of 224 × 224 pixels after
resizing. The dataset splits followed a stratified strategy with 70%
for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing, ensuring bal-
anced representation between classes (Table 2).
During training, all images were resized to 224 × 224 pixels, con-
verted to tensors, and normalized using standard ImageNet mean
and standard deviation values (mean = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
std = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]). To improve training variability, this
study employed augmentation techniques such as random hori-
zontal and vertical flips (p = 0.5 each), random rotations within
±15∘, and color jittering with controlled changes in brightness
(0.1), contrast (0.1), saturation (0.1) and hue (0.03). To mitigate
class imbalance, this work employed a weighted random sam-
pling strategy, in which class-specific weights increase the contri-
bution of minority classes during optimization, thereby improv-
ing performance under class-imbalanced conditions [65].
The optimization of the SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model was per-
formed using the AdamW optimizer, chosen for its ability to
decouple weight decay from learning rate. The initial learning
rate was set to 2 × 10−5. A cosine annealing schedule with warm
restarts was employed, with an initial cycle duration of 10 epochs,
a restart cycle multiplier of two, and a minimum learning rate of
1 × 10−6, to encourage periodic exploration of new minima and
reduce the risk of premature convergence [66] (Figure 3). Finally,
to improve robustness and reduce overconfident predictions, the
entropy was regularized with strength 𝜆= 0.01. This encouraged
the model to maintain uncertainty where appropriate, limiting
overfitting to noisy histopathology patterns.
The proposed model was implemented in python (v3.10) using
PyTorch (v2.2), with supporting libraries including TorchMetrics
for evaluation, LIME and SHAP for explainability. Training was
performed on an NVIDIA L4 GPU (24 GB VRAM) with mixed
precision enabled.

hybrid SwinTrasDConvNeXt model on the multi-classification of

The primary objective of this study was to assess the effec-
tiveness of the hybrid model in the multi-class classification of
eight breast cancer subtypes from histopathology images under
challenging conditions of data imbalance and morphological
variability.

Model Training Dynamics
and Convergence
The proposed hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model utilized in
this study achieved superior performance in 50 epochs. The
model suggested a stable generalization to unseen data, as
the validation accuracy improved from 69.22% to a peak of
92.33% at epoch 10, after which it oscillated within the range of
93%–95%. Similarly, its training accuracy improved from an ini-
tial 46.93%–98.09% by the final epoch. In contrast, the model’s
validation loss steadily declined from 0.5144 to 0.1458, while the
training loss dropped from 0.7823 to 0.0291 by the final epoch.
In general, the model effectively learned to discriminate fea-
tures from training data while avoiding overfitting, as shown in
Figure 3.
Figure 4 presents the dynamics of the gating mechanism and
backbone preference between training epochs. The alpha mean
evolution in Figure 4a shows a consistence increases from
approximately 0.27 to around 0.43 over 50 epochs, indicating
a progressive strengthening of the gating mechanism towards
balanced feature integration. Similarly, Figure 4b illustrates the
backbone preference distribution between the ConvNeXt and
Swin Transformer models. To encourage balanced contributions
from Swin Tansformer and ConvNeXt, the mean activation of
the fusion gate (𝛼) was regularized using a L2 penalty towards
0.5. This prevented the gate from collapsing entirely in one back-
bone, ensuring that both models had a fair opportunity to con-
tribute during training. Although both Swin Transformer and
ConvNeXt parameters were trained with the same learning rate
(2 × 105), fusion gate statistics showed an early dominance of
Swin Transformer features (99.8%) and then gradually incor-
porated ConvNeXt. This preference indicates that Transformer
features (global) are more stable at the beginning, while Con-
vNeXt’s CNN filters (local) need more fine-tuning to specialize,
mimicking how medical experts reason visually by starting with
global context (gist of abnormality) and then zooming in to local
details [67].
4.2
|
Results
This section summarize the quantitative results obtained on the
training, validation, and testing sets. Our proposed hybrid model
distinguishes itself on all sets by dynamically fusing local fea-
tures from ConvNeXt with broad contextual cues derived from
the Swin Transformer’s self-attention mechanism via a learnable
parameter. To contextualize the results, we report the overall pro-
portion of correct predictions (accuracy), the quantity of how
many positively predicted cases are truly positive (Precision), the
fraction of actual positives that are detected (Recall), the frac-
tion of actual negatives correctly identified (Specificity), a bal-
ance between precision and recall (F1 Score), and the area under
the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). All results
reported on the BreakHis dataset for the proposed method cor-
respond to a multiclass classification setting that aggregates all
magnification levels (40×, 100×, 200×, and 400×) within each
tumor subclass.
Table 3 presents the overall performance of the proposed
SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF hybrid model on the training, validation,
and test sets. The model demonstrated consistently strong perfor-
mance across all evaluation metrics, suggesting robustness and
effective generalization. Most importantly, on the test set, the
proposed SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model achieved 95.53% accu-
racy, 94.74% precision, 96.32% recall, and 95.47% F1 score, con-
firming its robustness. The slightly higher recall relative to pre-
cision on the test set indicates that the model favored sensitivity,
demonstrating robustness in capturing a large proportion of true
positives while still maintaining strong precision. Furthermore,
the model was evaluated using five folds, in which four folds
were used for training and validation and the remaining fold for
testing.
Across the four folds in Table 4, accuracy ranged from 86.9%
to 91.9%, while the area under the AUC remained consis-
tently high (0.990–0.995), reflecting the strong discrimina-
tive ability across class boundaries. The precision-recall AUC

Confusion Matrix and Receiver Operating
Characteristic (ROC) Analysis
Figure 5 presents the confusion matrices of the proposed hybrid
model on the training, validation, and test sets for the eight
breast cancer subtypes. The confusion matrices demonstrate that
the model achieved high accuracy per class, with most predic-
tions concentrated along the diagonal, reflecting correct clas-
sifications. On the training set shown in Figure 5a, almost all
classes were classified with minimal misclassification, indicat-
ing strong discriminative learning by the model. The validation
and test set results desmontrated a small number of off-diagonal
misclassifications, particularly among histologically similar sub-
types such as fibroadenoma and phyllodes tumor as shown in
Figure 5b and c, respectively.
The ROC curves for each dataset in Figure 6 further confirm
the discriminative power of the model, with all classes achiev-
ing AUC close to 1.0, indicating favorable sensitivity–specificity
trade-offs for both majority and minority classes. Notably, the test
set ROC curves maintained consistently high AUC values across
all subtypes, demonstrating the stability of the proposed model
on unseen data.
4.2.2
|
Class-Wise Performance Metrics
Figure 7 illustrates the classification performance of the hybrid
SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model, evaluated on BreakHis dataset
across eight breast tumor subclasses using precision, recall, and
F1-score as primary metrics. Overall, the model demonstrated
consistently high predictive accuracy, with most subclasses
achieving above 94% across all metrics. For benign subclasses,
adenosis and tubular adenomas exhibited a nearly-perfect clas-
sification, with scores exceeding 97% for precision, recall, and F1
score. Fibroadenoma and phyllodes tumor also achieved strong
performance (precision 91%–96%; recall 94%–95%; F1 Score
93%–95%), indicating robust separability of these histopatholog-
ical patterns. Among malignant subclasses, ductal carcinoma,
the most prevalent clinical subtype, achieved a high precision
of 97.14%, a recall of 100.00%, and an F1-score of 98.55%. Papil-
lary carcinoma also demonstrated strong performance, achieving
97.62% across all evaluation metrics, while mucinous carcinomas
were classified with metrics performance ranging from 94% to
95%, reflecting the model’s ability to capture their distinctive mor-
phological features.

In contrast, lobular carcinoma posed the greatest classification
challenge. Although recall remained relatively high (93.68%),
precision was comparatively lower (81.65%), resulting in the
lowest F1-score of 87.25% among all subclasses. The confu-
sion matrix in Figure 5c indicates that lobular carcinoma cases
were frequently misclassified as ductal carcinoma, highlight-
ing substantial morphological overlap between these two enti-
ties. Furthermore, the confusion matrix also indicates that
mucinous carcinomas were occasionally misclassified as duc-
tal carcinoma, reflecting the histopathological similarity of these
subtypes in certain regions of the tissue (mixed mucinous
carcinoma).
This imbalance indicates that, although the model was able to
correctly identify most of the cases of lobular and mucinous
carcinomas, it also produced a notable number of false-positive
classifications, thereby reducing precision. Such challenges are
consistent with clinical observations, since ILC often exhibits
subtle, diffuse growth patterns that overlap with IDC and other
subtypes, while mucinous carcinoma can share morphological
similarities with ductal carcinoma, further complicating reliable
discrimination [68, 69].
Overall, consistently strong per-class performance metrics rein-
force the ability of the proposed SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model
to generalize across various subtypes of breast tumors, while
also highlighting specific challenges in subclasses such as lobular
carcinomas.

Figure 10 illustrates the class-level SHAP analysis which reveals
that the model’s predictions were mainly driven by duc-
tal carcinoma (40.7%) and adenosis (40.0%), which together
represented more than 80% of the overall contribution to
decision-making. This strong weighting suggests that the hybrid
SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF effectively captured the distinctive mor-
phological patterns of these subtypes, making them dominant
drivers of classification.
In contrast, subtypes such as lobular carcinoma contributed
11.2% to the reasoning of the model. Fibroadenoma, papillary
carcinoma, phyllodes tumor, tubular adenoma, and mucinous
carcinoma played a relatively minor role by contributing only
1.64% on average. These lower contributions may reflect subtle
histopathological variations or a limited representation of these
classes within the dataset, making their discriminatory features
less influential during training.
Overall, the SHAP findings indicate that the hybrid model priori-
tized the most morphologically distinct subtypes while still lever-
aging auxiliary contributions from rarer and visually overlapping
classes, thereby improving robustness in multi-class histopathol-
ogy classification.

Discussion
This work demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed
hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF architecture in addressing the

challenging task of global–local feature extraction and fusion
for classification of breast cancer subtypes using histopatholog-
ical images. By integrating the global modeling capabilities of
the Swin Transformer with the strong local feature extraction of
ConvNeXt, the hybrid model achieved robust performance on
training, validation, and test sets. As shown in Tables 3 and 4,
the model consistently achieved high accuracy, AUC, precision,
recall, and F1-scores, with cross-validation confirming its sta-
bility and generalization. The proposed hybrid approach proved
particularly effective in distinguishing common breast cancer
subtypes on the BreakHis dataset, including ductal carcinoma,
adenosis, fibroadenoma, and phyllodes tumor, where precision
and recall exceeded 95% when evaluated in a multiclass set-
ting that aggregates all magnification levels. This strong perfor-
mance can be attributed to the synergy of global context mod-
eling and fine-grained local pattern recognition, which mirrors
the diagnostic reasoning process of pathologists, first surveying
the global tissue architecture before focusing on localized cellu-
lar details. Additional evaluation on the BACH dataset further
demonstrated the adaptability of the proposed architecture to a
related histopathology classification task, supporting its robust-
ness across different datasets.
Explainability analysis using LIME and SHAP further validates
model predictions, showing that the hybrid network consis-
tently focused on clinically substantial histopathological features.
SHAP analysis highlighted that adenosis and ductal carcinoma
were the most influential subtypes driving predictions, while
rarer or morphologically overlapping classes such as fibroade-
noma and tubular adenoma contributed less prominently. This
alignment between model reasoning and known pathological
distinctions reinforces confidence in the ability of the proposed
hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF model to prioritize biologically
relevant patterns during classification.
Beyond predictive performance, computational efficiency is an
important consideration for practical deployment. As summa-
rized in Table 1, the computational cost of the proposed hybrid
model is primarily dominated by the feature extraction stages
of the ConvNeXt-Tiny and Swin-Tiny backbones. Although
Swin-Tiny introduces a quadratic attention term within local win-
dows, its window-based design effectively constrains this cost
and preserves scalability. In contrast, the learnable dynamic gat-
ing fusion and post-fusion refinement modules incur only linear
computational overhead, ensuring that the proposed fusion strat-
egy remains efficient while enabling effective global–local fea-
ture integration.
These findings underscore the potential of hybrid architectures
for clinically aligned classification of breast cancer subtypes.
However, performance gaps in rare and subtle morphologi-
cal subtypes suggest directions for future work. These include
(i) subtype-specific feature learning and uncertainty model-
ing, and (ii) integration of larger, multi-institutional datasets to
further enhance robustness and clinical applicability. Beyond
histopathology, the proposed hybrid framework has the potential
for adaptation to other imaging modalities such as mammogra-
phy, ultrasound, and MRI, with modality-specific retraining.

Conclusions
In this study, we developed and rigorously evaluated a hybrid
SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF framework for the classification of eight
breast cancer subtypes using the BreaKHis histopathology
dataset. By leveraging the strengths of global context mod-
eling from Swin Transformer and fine-grained local feature
extraction from ConvNeXt, the hybrid model achieved strong
and consistent performance across training, validation, and test

sets. Cross-validation further confirmed the robustness of the
model, with mean test accuracy exceeding 91% and threshold-free
metrics such as AUC and PR-AUC consistently above 99%.
Confusion-matrix analysis showed highly accurate recognition of
subtypes such as adenosis, fibroadenoma, ductal carcinoma, and
phyllodes tumor, while the most frequent confusions occurred
between lobular and mucinous carcinoma. Importantly, explain-
ability analysis using LIME and lesion area quantification con-
firmed that the model was attentive to histologically significant
tumor regions, aligning its decision-making with clinical reason-
ing. This not only increases confidence in the results of the model,
but also demonstrates its potential as a clinically interpretable
decision-support tool for pathologists.
While the proposed hybrid SwinTConvNeXt-LDGF framework
demonstrats strong and consistent performance across multi-
ple evaluation settings, several limitations remain. In particu-
lar, reduced performance was observed for rare and morpho-
logically subtle breast cancer subtypes, highlighting challenges
associated with class imbalance and histopathological overlap.
Addressing these limitations will require future work focused on
subtype-specific feature learning, uncertainty-aware modeling.
In addition, incorporation of larger, multi-institutional datasets
will further enhance robustness and clinical applicability. More-
over, this study focused on histopathology, the proposed hybrid
architecture has the potential to be adapted to other breast imag-
ing modalities, such as mammography, ultrasound, and MRI,
with appropriate modality-specific retraining.
